data.title: Air Products and Chemicals raises dividend by 1.1% to $1.81
data.description:
• Air Products and Chemicals (APD) declares $1.81/share quarterly dividend, 1.1% increase from prior dividend of $1.79.
  • Forward yield 2.44%
  • Payable Aug. 10; for shareholders of record July 1; ex-div July 1.
  • See APD Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Pot stocks rise as Trump reschedules marijuana as a less-dangerous drug: AP
data.description:
Shares of cannabis companies rose in the premarket on Thursday after the Associated Press reported President Donald Trump’s acting attorney general signed an order rescheduling state-licensed medical marijuana as a less-dangerous drug.

Major gainers include Canadian Licensed Producers such as Canopy Growth (CGC), Tilray (TLRY), Cronos (CRON), Aurora Cannabis (ACB), SNDL (SNDL), and OrganiGram Holdings (OGI).

Stocks to watch: U.S. MSOs including Curaleaf Holdings (CURLF), Cresco Labs (CRLBF), Green Thumb (GTBIF), Trulieve Cannabis (TCNNF), Ayr Wellness (AYRWF), TerrAscend (TSNDF), Verano Holdings (VRNO), and Village Farms International (VFF)

Cannabis-focused ETFs: AdvisorShares Pure Cannabis ETF (YOLO), ETFMG Alternative Harvest ETF (MJ), Amplify Seymour Cannabis ETF (CNBS), AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS)

The order signed by Todd Blanche does not legalize marijuana for medical or recreational use under U.S. law. But it does change the way it's regulated, shifting licensed medical marijuana from Schedule I — reserved for drugs without medical use and with high potential for abuse — to the less strictly regulated Schedule III. It also gives licensed medical marijuana operators a major tax break and eases some barriers to researching cannabis.

Trump told his administration in December to work as quickly as possible to reclassify marijuana. On Saturday, as the Republican president signed an unrelated executive order about psychedelics, he seemed to express frustration that it was taking so long.

Blanche said Thursday that the Department of Justice was “delivering on President Trump’s promise” to expand Americans’ access to medical treatment options. “This rescheduling action allows for research on the safety and efficacy of this substance, ultimately providing patients with better care and doctors with more reliable information,” he said in a statement.

Blanche's action Iargely legitimizes medical marijuana programs in the 40 states that have adopted them. It sets up an expedited system for state-licensed medical marijuana producers and distributors to register with the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration.

It makes clear that cannabis researchers won't be penalized for obtaining state-licensed marijuana or marijuana-derived products for use in their work, and it grants state-licensed medical marijuana companies a windfall by allowing them, for the first time, to deduct business expenses on their federal taxes.

Any marijuana-derived medicine approved by the Food and Drug Administration is similarly listed in Schedule III, it said.

The order represents a major policy shift for the U.S. government, which has continued its longstanding marijuana prohibition — dating to the Marijuana Tax Act of 1937 — even as nearly all the states have approved cannabis use in some form. Two dozen states plus Washington, D.C., have authorized adult recreational use of marijuana, 40 have medical marijuana systems, and eight others allow low-THC cannabis or CBD oil for medical use. Only Idaho and Kansas ban marijuana outright.

The regulation of medical marijuana has come a long way since California became the first state to adopt it in 1996, Blanche wrote.

“Today the vast majority of States maintain comprehensive licensing frameworks governing cultivation, processing, distribution, and dispensing of marijuana for medical purposes,” Blanche wrote. “Taken as a whole, they demonstrate a sustained capacity to achieve the public-interest objectives ... including protecting public health and safety and preventing the diversion of controlled substances into illicit channels.”

Marijuana or marijuana-derived products that are not distributed through a state medical marijuana program will continue to be classified in Schedule I. The Trump administration is launching a new administrative hearing process beginning in June to consider the broader rescheduling of marijuana.

Schedule III drugs are defined as having moderate to low potential for physical and psychological dependence. Some critics of the industry have suggested that legalization in the states has led to stronger and stronger cannabis products, which need to be researched rather than categorized less strictly than before.

The Justice Department under President Joe Biden, a Democrat, had proposed to reclassify marijuana, eliciting nearly 43,000 formal public comments. The Drug Enforcement Administration was still in the review process when Trump succeeded Biden in January, and Trump ordered that process to move along as quickly as legally possible.

Blanche's order sidestepped the review process by relying on a provision of federal law that allows the attorney general to determine the appropriate classification for drugs that the U.S. must regulate pursuant to an international treaty.

It was unclear how the order might affect operations in states where licensed recreational marijuana shops also sell to medical patients. In Washington state, which in 2012 became one of the first states to legalize the adult use of marijuana, 302 of 460 licensed stores have endorsements allowing them to sell tax-free cannabis products to registered patients.

Many Republicans oppose loosening marijuana restrictions. More than 20 Republican senators, several of them staunch Trump allies, signed a letter last year urging the president to keep the current standards.

Trump has made his crusade against other drugs, especially fentanyl, a feature of his second term, ordering U.S. military attacks on Venezuelan and other boats the administration insists are ferrying drugs. He signed another executive order declaring fentanyl a weapon of mass destruction.

The AP contributed to this report.

data.title: Freeport McMoRan falls as Grasberg delay cuts 2026 output outlook
data.description:
Freeport McMoRan (FCX), one of the world’s largest publicly traded copper producers, reported results on Thursday that beat Wall Street expectations for revenue and adjusted earnings, but shares fell sharply after the company reduced 2026 sales forecasts because of delays at its key Grasberg mine in Indonesia.

Freeport McMoRan (FCX) stock fell 8.4% in premarket trading. As of Wednesday’s close, the stock was up 107% over the prior 12 months.

Revenue rose to $6.23 billion from $5.73 billion a year earlier, topping analysts’ consensus estimate of $5.96 billion.

Adjusted net income attributable to common stock was $830 million, or $0.57 a share, above estimates of $0.47 a share.

Net income rose to $881 million, or $0.61 a share, from $352 million, or $0.24 a share, a year earlier.

Why shares are dropping

Despite the earnings beat, investors appeared focused on weaker production guidance tied to operational bottlenecks at Grasberg Block Cave, one of the company’s most valuable assets.

Freeport McMoRan (FCX) cut its 2026 copper sales forecast to 3.1 billion pounds from a January estimate of 3.4 billion pounds. Gold sales guidance was reduced to 650,000 ounces (24,569.59 kg) from 800,000 ounces (30,239.49 kg).

The company said the ramp-up of Grasberg Block Cave has been slowed by wet ore conditions and required modifications to ore loading infrastructure. Near-term production from key blocks is expected to run at about 60% of capacity until upgrades are completed.

Production rates once expected to reach 85% of capacity in the second half of 2026 are now forecast at about 65%, with full capacity pushed closer to late 2027.

CEO stresses long-term strength

“Our first quarter financial results reflect the strength of our diversified portfolio with growth in revenues, cash flow and earnings, compared with last year’s first quarter, despite reduced capacity at our Indonesia operations,” Chief Executive Kathleen Quirk said in the earnings release.

Quirk added that the company remains strongly positioned as “America’s Copper Champion” with geographically diverse operations and a pipeline of growth projects.

Cash flow strong, expansion plans advance

Operating cash flow rose to $1.5 billion from $1.06 billion a year earlier, while capital expenditures totaled $973 million. Freeport McMoRan (FCX) ended the quarter with $3.7 billion in cash and net debt of $2.4 billion, excluding project debt tied to Indonesian downstream facilities.

The company said it continues advancing organic growth plans, including a possible major expansion at El Abra in Chile and brownfield growth projects in Arizona using new leaching technologies.

Freeport McMoRan (FCX) operates major copper, gold and molybdenum mines including the Grasberg minerals district in Indonesia, Morenci in Arizona, Cerro Verde in Peru, Bagdad in Arizona and El Abra in Chile. The company is a major supplier of copper used in power grids, electric vehicles, data centers and industrial infrastructure.

data.title: High Roller Technologies signs marketing agreement with Leverage Game Media
data.description:
• High Roller Technologies (ROLR) has executed a definitive strategic marketing partnership agreement with Leverage Game Media to support customer acquisition and brand awareness initiatives tied to High Roller’s planned launch of a regulated event-based prediction markets product in the United States, the company said on Thursday.
  • Under the terms of the agreement, Leverage Game Media will leverage its digital content platforms, data-driven marketing capabilities, and social media audience to support High Roller’s prediction markets growth strategy. 
  • ROLR +0.68% premarket to $8.5583.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Chicago Fed National Activity Index slumps in March
data.description:
March Chicago Fed National Activity Index: -0.20 vs. +0.03 in February (revised from -11), the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago said on Thursday.

CFNAI three-month average: -0.03 vs. +0.03 prior (revised from -0.01).

data.title: Ladder Capital Non-GAAP EPS of $0.22 in-line, revenue of $51.88M misses by $0.39M
data.description:
• Ladder Capital press release (LADR): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.22 in-line.
  • Revenue of $51.88M (+1.3% Y/Y) misses by $0.39M.
  • On April 21, 2026, the board approved repurchasing $100.0 million of Class A common stock, increasing the total authority from $77.2 million to $100.0 million.

data.title: Jefferson Capital secures credit facility expansion
data.description:
• Jefferson Capital (JCAP) announced on Thursday the successful expansion of its revolving credit facility by $150M, bringing total capacity to $1.15B. The expansion adds two new banking partners to the lending syndicate, each committing $75M.
  • The company added that the amended credit agreement also increases the maximum cap on the aggregate amount to which the revolving credit commitments may be increased in the future to $1.425B.  The amendment did not include any other material changes.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Snap-on revenue tops estimates, but earnings miss as margins tighten
data.description:
Snap-on (SNA), the maker of premium mechanic tools and equipment, reported results on Thursday that narrowly topped Wall Street revenue expectations but missed on earnings. Shares of Snap-on (SNA) were little changed in premarket trading. As of Wednesday’s close, the stock was up 27% over the prior 12 months.

Net sales rose 5.8% to $1.21 billion from $1.14 billion a year earlier, narrowly beating analysts’ consensus estimate of $1.18 billion.

Net income rose to $247.0 million, or $4.69 a share, from $240.5 million, or $4.51 a share, a year earlier, and missed estimates of $4.75 a share.

Margins slip despite higher sales

Gross margin declined to 50.4% from 50.7% a year earlier, while consolidated operating margin fell to 24.4% from 25.2%. The company said foreign currency effects reduced gross margin by 40 basis points.

Operating earnings increased to $318.8 million from $313.4 million, but profit growth lagged the pace of revenue gains, helping explain the earnings shortfall.

“Our first quarter was encouraging, led by robust sales growth with customers in critical industries and improved activity in the U.S. Tools Group,” Chairman and Chief Executive Nick Pinchuk said in the earnings release.

Tools business strength offsets mixed repair trends

Snap-on tools group sales rose 5.0% to $486.0 million, with operating margin improving to 21.6% from 20.0%, helped by stronger U.S. and international activity.

Commercial and industrial group sales climbed 11% to $381.0 million, led by demand from critical industries and specialty torque products.

Repair systems and information group sales rose 2.0% to $485.3 million, though operating earnings slipped to $119.5 million from $122.1 million as weaker dealership demand offset gains with independent repair shops.

Cash flow improves, outlook steady

Snap-on (SNA) generated $368.7 million in operating cash flow, up from $298.5 million a year earlier, ending the quarter with $1.75 billion in cash.

The company said it expects to continue investing in growth initiatives during 2026 and projected about $100 million in capital expenditures for the full year.

Snap-on (SNA) is best known for the red tool chests and hand tools sold from its mobile franchise vans to professional mechanics, along with Mitchell1 repair software, diagnostics scanners, torque systems and industrial tools used in aerospace, military and manufacturing markets.

data.title: Nokia targets 12%-14% 2026 Network Infrastructure growth as it raises Optical+IP outlook to 18%-20%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nokia (NOK) Q1 2026

Management view

  • President and CEO Justin Hotard framed Q1 as a stronger start driven by Network Infrastructure and AI/cloud demand: "Net sales grew 4% to EUR 4.5 billion with an operating margin of 6.2%, and we delivered a free cash flow of EUR 629 million in the quarter." He also tied profitability to mix and prior-year items, saying gross margin expansion was "supported in part by the absence of a onetime charge in mobile infrastructure in the prior year" and by "strong performance in Optical Networks as we began to see the synergy benefits from the Infinera acquisition."

  • CEO Hotard emphasized acceleration in AI-driven infrastructure spending expectations and Nokia’s revised market view: "At the time, expectations were for the largest hyperscalers to spend around $540 billion in CapEx in 2026. Now, those expectations have increased to over $700 billion." He added, "We now expect our AI and cloud addressable market to grow at a 27% CAGR between 2025 and 2028, up from the 16% we shared in November."

  • CEO Hotard highlighted optical product roadmap timing and claimed differentiation, including that Nokia’s "next-generation hyperscale multi-rail solution" will "begin shipping later this year" and that a building-block architecture will "begin sampling in the first half of 2027 and will ship in volume in the second half" with "a reduced total cost of ownership of up to 70%."

  • Chief Financial Officer Marco Wiren summarized segment drivers and balance-sheet position: "Free cash flow was EUR 629 million, and the quarter ended with a net cash of EUR 3.8 billion." He also said Optical Networks growth was "mainly driven by AI and cloud customers" and that IP Networks softness outside AI/cloud should improve: "We expect growth in IP Networks to start to accelerate in quarter 2 as we ramp shipments tied to new design wins with AI and cloud customers."

Outlook

  • Management raised Network Infrastructure growth assumptions for 2026 while keeping the group operating profit range unchanged. CFO Wiren said: "Our group level financial outlook remains unchanged" and reiterated the comparable operating profit range of "between EUR 2 billion and EUR 2.5 billion."

  • CFO Wiren detailed the revised segment targets: "We now target to grow faster in Network Infrastructure this year with 12% to 14% growth, up from the previous assumption of 6% to 8%." He added: "specifically in Optical and IP Networks, we now target 18% to 20% growth, up from the previous 10% to 12% growth."

  • For nearer-term phasing, CFO Wiren said: "regarding quarter 2, we currently assume a 5% to 9% sequential increase in net sales" and "we expect quarter 2 to account for between 12% and 16% of the full year" operating profit.

Financial results

  • CEO Hotard reported Q1 profitability and cash generation, including "gross margin expanded 320 basis points" and "Operating profit was EUR 281 million." He also said group book-to-bill was "above 1" and that Network Infrastructure book-to-bill was "well above 1."

  • CFO Wiren reported Network Infrastructure performance and margin context: "Network Infrastructure sales grew 6% in quarter 1" and "Gross margin in Network Infrastructure was 43.4%, increasing 150 basis points," while noting investment and mix dynamics: "We continue to expect some gross margin headwinds through the year as a result of product mix" and "we had a full quarter of Infinera expenses compared to 1 month last year."

  • CFO Wiren described Mobile Infrastructure quarterly profitability and seasonality: "Gross margin increased by 430 basis points to 48.5%" and "Operating margin was 8.9% in the quarter," while cautioning: "We expect Mobile Infrastructure gross margins in the second and third quarters to be somewhat weaker and then much stronger in quarter 4."

Q&A

  • Fredrik Lithell, Handelsbanken: asked what drove the raised Optical/IP growth assumptions; President and CEO Hotard said it was "a little bit more confidence on supply" and "some of the traction we're starting to see in IP networking" with "more visibility for the year."

  • Janardan Menon, Jefferies: asked whether the EUR 1B new orders included next-gen OFC products; President and CEO Hotard said 2026 momentum is "on the back of our 800-gig pluggable" and described "some elongation in orders" toward "a longer-term commitment on orders." He later added the 18%-20% outlook is "across both sides of the business."

  • Artem Beletski, SEB: asked about delivery constraints and Fixed Networks headwinds; President and CEO Hotard referenced "constraint in the semiconductor ecosystem" and said the company will "continue to be disciplined" in CPE, expecting "some headwind on the CPE side" while shifting to "a long-term sustainable growth profile" with emphasis on "gross margin and operating profit."

  • Robert Sanders, Deutsche Bank: asked about Optical profitability targets and OpEx; CFO Wiren said, "we aim for double-digit operating margins, and this is something we are still believing in" and noted focus on investing to secure constrained supply, adding, "Otherwise, we don't guide any specific OpEx numbers."

  • Richard Kramer, Arete: asked whether the EUR 1B orders were firm POs or longer-term frames; President and CEO Hotard said, "the only thing you see in orders is firm purchase orders with delivery dates" and called elongation "a net positive" for planning. Kramer also asked about cash drains; CFO Wiren responded, "we are quite confident about our cash position" and reiterated capital allocation priorities including that "If we deem to have excess capital, then we can consider share buybacks as well."

  • Felix Henriksson, Nordea: asked if pricing drove the guidance upgrade and lead times; President and CEO Hotard said growth was "much more unit volume" and later gave a lead-time view: "a good way to think about the broader lead times we're seeing today" is "12 to 18 months."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive and opportunity-focused, with repeated probing on supply constraints, order visibility, and margin/OpEx trade-offs; questions centered on whether the raised growth assumptions were driven by capacity, pricing, or backlog conversion.

  • Management tone was slightly positive and execution-focused in prepared remarks, while measured in Q&A on constraints and customer disclosures; President and CEO Hotard repeatedly prioritized delivery and scaling, saying, "My priority is making sure we're partnering with them effectively" and on book-to-bill, "Our focus right now is on just maximizing the demand."

  • Versus the prior quarter, the discussion shifted from balanced/“disciplined” guidance language to explicit upside in Network Infrastructure growth assumptions; Q&A also intensified around supply chain maturity and how incremental investment keeps group profit guidance unchanged.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language tightened around higher Network Infrastructure growth: in Q4, CEO Hotard said for 2026, "we expect growth rates... to be in line with these long-term targets" (6%-8% NI and 10%-12% Optical+IP), while in Q1, CFO Wiren raised 2026 assumptions to "12% to 14%" for NI and "18% to 20%" for Optical+IP.

  • The “investment vs. margin” theme carried over, but Q1 added clearer commentary that higher NI growth would be supported by "investing to capture the long-term growth opportunity," while keeping group operating profit range unchanged.

  • Analyst focus evolved from Q4 questions about conservatism and sub-seasonality to Q1 questions about the firmness of the EUR 1B orders, optical lead times, pricing, and the durability of Infinera synergy-driven profitability.

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited supply constraints and semiconductor lead times as limiting factors in scaling AI-driven demand; President and CEO Hotard said there is "a fair amount of constraint in the semiconductor ecosystem" and described industrywide needs to "build capacity" across optical components and subsystems.

  • Fixed Networks portfolio discipline was described as a near-term revenue headwind; President and CEO Hotard said Nokia expects to "continue to have some headwind on the CPE side" while focusing on areas "where it's valued" and improving "gross margin and operating profit."

  • CFO Wiren flagged intra-year profitability variability in Mobile Infrastructure, stating gross margins in Q2 and Q3 are expected to be "somewhat weaker" before being "much stronger in quarter 4."

Final takeaway

Nokia’s management presented Q1 as a stronger start to 2026, anchored by Optical Networks momentum, early Infinera synergy benefits, and accelerating AI/cloud demand, while raising 2026 Network Infrastructure growth assumptions to 12%-14% and Optical+IP to 18%-20% but keeping the group comparable operating profit range at EUR 2B to EUR 2.5B as it invests in R&D, production capability, and supply-chain scaling amid acknowledged semiconductor and optical ecosystem constraints.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
